description:
<title>Validate restored JSON backups against their schemas and generate auditable reports</title>

    <description>
        You are working as a junior backup-operator who has just completed a test-restore of three
        application backup files.  Your team requires an auditable, machine-readable record showing:

          • whether every restored JSON document still satisfies its JSON schema  
          • that the validation was performed concurrently (for speed)  
          • a human-readable log for each file plus an aggregated JSON summary  

        The restore procedure has already copied all data and schema files into the
        following locations (do NOT move them):

          • JSON payloads:      /home/user/backups/finance.json  
                                /home/user/backups/hr.json  
                                /home/user/backups/it.json  

          • JSON schemas:       /home/user/schemas/finance_schema.json  
                                /home/user/schemas/hr_schema.json  
                                /home/user/schemas/it_schema.json  

        Your tasks:

        1. Create the directory /home/user/restore_logs if it does not already exist.  
        2. Validate every “*.json” file in /home/user/backups **in parallel** against its
           matching schema in /home/user/schemas.  
             • You may use jq in combination with bash logic, or any other CLI tools already
               present in a typical Debian/Ubuntu container.  
             • The schema files use a *very* simple structure: each contains a
               top-level array key called “required”.  A backup passes when **all**
               keys in that “required” array are present in the JSON payload  
               (exact value is irrelevant; only key existence matters).  
        3. Write a plain-text log called /home/user/restore_logs/validation_report.log.
           The file **must** contain exactly one line per backup file, formatted as:  

              <filename>: <PASS | FAIL> - <UTC-timestamp>  

           Formatting rules:  
             • <filename> is the base name, **without the directory path**  
             • A single colon, one space, then “PASS” or “FAIL” in all caps  
             • One space, a hyphen, one space, then the ISO-8601 UTC timestamp
               produced by `date -u "+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`  
             • End each line with a newline character “\n” (Unix LF)  
             • Lines may appear in any order.  
        4. Create a machine-readable summary file
           /home/user/restore_logs/consolidated_summary.json containing **exactly**:

              {
                "total": 3,
                "pass": 3,
                "fail": 0
              }

           Formatting rules for this summary JSON:  
             • Two-space indentation as shown above  
             • A newline at the end of the file  
             • The values must be integers, not strings.  
        5. Make sure that all validation commands were launched concurrently.
           (Tip: `xargs -P`, `parallel`, background jobs “&” + `wait`, etc.)

        The automated grader will check:  

          • /home/user/restore_logs/validation_report.log exists and contains three lines,
            each including the correct filename and the word PASS or FAIL.  
          • /home/user/restore_logs/consolidated_summary.json exists and matches the
            **exact** character-for-character content specified in step 4.  
          • At least one shell command line used parallelism (the grader inspects
            the user’s bash history / process tree).  

        If any backup does not satisfy its schema you must still list it in the log,
        mark it as FAIL, and adjust the JSON counters accordingly.

        When you are done, the only new artefacts should be the two files created in
        /home/user/restore_logs.  Do not rename, delete, or edit any of the supplied
        backup or schema files.
    </description>
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing payloads -->
    <file path="/home/user/backups/finance.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "id": "finance",
  "last_backup": "2024-01-15T10:31:52Z",
  "data": {
    "records": 15234,
    "checksum": "8f14e45fceea167a5a36dedd4bea2543"
  }
}
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/backups/hr.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "id": "hr",
  "last_backup": "2024-01-15T10:35:10Z",
  "data": {
    "records": 8921,
    "checksum": "e3afed0047b08059d0fada10f400c1e5"
  }
}
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/backups/it.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "id": "it",
  "last_backup": "2024-01-15T10:37:03Z",
  "data": {
    "records": 43210,
    "checksum": "1679091c5a880faf6fb5e6087eb1b2dc"
  }
}
]]></file>

    <!-- Pre-existing schemas -->
    <file path="/home/user/schemas/finance_schema.json"><![CDATA[
{ "required": ["id", "last_backup", "data"] }
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/schemas/hr_schema.json"><![CDATA[
{ "required": ["id", "last_backup", "data"] }
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/schemas/it_schema.json"><![CDATA[
{ "required": ["id", "last_backup", "data"] }
]]></file>

    <!-- Expected artefact created by the agent. 
         Timestamp fields are non-deterministic, so only the PASS/FAIL tokens are validated.
         The grader will apply a regex to confirm each required line exists. -->

    <expected_file path="/home/user/restore_logs/consolidated_summary.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "total": 3,
  "pass": 3,
  "fail": 0
}
]]></expected_file>
